title: Google Analytics Alternatives for 2026
description: A comparison of Google Analytics alternatives — Plausible, Fathom, Matomo, and Umami — covering privacy compliance, self-hosting options, data ownership, and analytics features for teams leaving GA4.
body:
The transition from Universal Analytics to Google Analytics 4 has prompted many teams to evaluate alternatives that offer simpler interfaces, built-in privacy compliance, and predictable pricing. The alternatives divide into two categories — lightweight privacy-focused analytics that prioritize simplicity and compliance, and full-featured platforms that match Google Analytics' reporting depth.

This comparison examines the major Google Analytics alternatives across data ownership, privacy compliance, analytics features, and pricing models. All pricing is based on published rates at the time of writing. Check each vendor's current pricing page for exact figures.

## Plausible

Plausible provides lightweight, privacy-focused web analytics without cookies or personal data collection. The dashboard shows pageviews, unique visitors, referrers, top pages, entry and exit pages, and goal conversions. The platform is GDPR-compliant without requiring a cookie consent banner.

Plausible's advantage is the simplicity. The dashboard loads quickly and shows the essential metrics without navigation through submenus and filtered views. The weekly email report provides a snapshot of traffic trends without logging into the dashboard.

The limitation is the analytics depth. Plausible does not support event tracking, user segmentation, funnel analysis, or custom dimensions. Teams that need behavior-level analysis beyond aggregate pageview data will find Plausible insufficient.

## Fathom

Fathom provides privacy-focused analytics with a similar approach to Plausible — cookie-free tracking, simple dashboards, and pageview-based pricing. Fathom differentiates through its self-hosting option, available as a one-time license fee for teams that prefer to host analytics data on their own infrastructure.

Fathom's advantage is the data ownership through self-hosting. Teams with compliance requirements that mandate data residency or complete control over analytics infrastructure can run Fathom on their own servers without ongoing subscription costs beyond infrastructure maintenance.

The limitation mirrors Plausible: aggregate-level analytics without the event tracking or user segmentation that Google Analytics provides.

## Matomo

Matomo provides a full-featured analytics platform with a self-hosted option. Matomo On-Premise is self-hosted with complete data ownership and a one-time license fee. Matomo Cloud is a hosted version with monthly subscription pricing.

Matomo's advantage is the feature parity with Google Analytics. Matomo provides pageview tracking, event tracking, goal conversion, ecommerce tracking, heatmaps, session recordings, A/B testing, and custom reports. Teams migrating from Google Analytics can replicate their existing reporting in Matomo with similar depth.

The limitation is the complexity. Matomo's feature set requires more configuration than Plausible or Fathom, and the self-hosted option requires infrastructure maintenance for the database, application, and cron job processing.

Check Matomo's current pricing page for exact rates on the cloud-hosted plans and the On-Premise license.

## Umami

Umami provides a lightweight, self-hosted analytics platform with a focus on simplicity and data ownership. The platform tracks pageviews, events, referrers, and visitor information with a clean dashboard. Umami is completely free and open source.

Umami's advantage is the zero-cost self-hosting. Teams with Docker experience can deploy Umami on a small server or cloud instance in minutes, providing their own analytics infrastructure without per-pageview costs or user limits.

The limitation is the feature set. Umami provides aggregate analytics with basic event tracking but lacks the advanced reporting, segmentation, and integration capabilities of Matomo or Google Analytics.

## Comparison Summary

The Google Analytics alternative choice depends on the team's analytics requirements and infrastructure capabilities. Teams that need simple, privacy-compliant analytics for content sites should evaluate Plausible for its hosted simplicity or Fathom for its self-hosting option. Teams that need feature parity with Google Analytics — custom dimensions, event tracking, user segmentation — should evaluate Matomo for its comprehensive feature set. Teams that want a free, self-hosted solution with minimal configuration should evaluate Umami.

The migration from Google Analytics takes one to two weeks depending on the tracking implementation and reporting complexity. Teams should run the new analytics platform in parallel with Google Analytics for at least one full reporting cycle to validate data accuracy before removing the Google Analytics tracking code.
author: PilotStack Team
